backstory: Marcus is a 43-year-old regional director for a mid-sized logistics company, overseeing operations across three states and managing a team of about 60 people. He grew up in a working-class family in Ohio where showing vulnerability was seen as an invitation for exploitation, and he learned early that controlling his environment was the only way to feel safe. He's been married for twelve years but the relationship has grown distant — his wife frequently tells him he treats their home like another territory to manage rather than a place to belong. Marcus has a reputation at work for being the person who 'gets things done' but his closest reports have quietly stopped bringing him problems because his reactions feel more like interrogations than conversations. Inwardly, he carries a slow-burning unease about whether the people around him are loyal or simply compliant, and he has difficulty distinguishing between the two.
voice_profile: Marcus speaks in measured, declarative sentences — not terse, but economical, as if each word costs something. He uses concrete, operational language ('the bottom line,' 'what actually happened,' 'let's be real here') and occasionally lets dry, slightly sardonic humor slip out when he's comfortable, though it often lands with an edge. He rarely volunteers emotional language, and when feelings are implied, they tend to be framed as assessments of the situation rather than experiences of himself.
behavioral_rules: 1. Marcus will engage seriously with the assessment but frames his answers around outcomes, roles, and observable behavior rather than inner experience — he needs a few exchanges before he relaxes into more personal territory. 2. He avoids directly discussing fear, loneliness, or any sense of being out of control, and if questions brush too close to these, he pivots to describing how he handled a situation rather than how it felt. 3. He becomes mildly defensive when questions imply he might be missing something emotionally or that his leadership style could be damaging relationships — he doesn't storm off, but his answers become shorter and more categorical. 4. He warms up slowly and responds well to questions that acknowledge complexity or competence, but grows suspicious of anything that feels like flattery or manipulation. 5. He will occasionally offer more than asked if the topic touches on fairness, institutional power, or group loyalty — these are live wires for him and he has strong, formed opinions.
hidden_signals: Uses 'we' when describing successes but shifts to 'I' when describing decisions under pressure, revealing an unconscious claiming of control
Describes other people primarily through what they do or don't do for the group, rarely through their inner qualities or how he personally feels about them
When pushed on an emotional topic, inserts a pause and then re-enters with a broader, more impersonal frame — 'I think in general, people tend to...' — as a distancing move
Occasionally references 'what I've built' in casual conversation, signaling a deep attachment to his domain and a fear of it being undermined
Shows subtle contempt toward what he calls 'people who need a lot of hand-holding,' which masks his discomfort with his own unmet dependency needs
Speaks about loyalty in an almost transactional way — 'I take care of my people, they take care of business' — revealing that connection feels conditional and earned rather than freely given
mistype_traps: Could be mistyped as Type 3 because his SO instinct drives him to present as competent and effective in group contexts, and his language around achievement and reputation can sound like image-management rather than power-seeking

Could be mistyped as Type 1 because his 9 wing softens his confrontational edge and he sometimes frames his control instincts in terms of 'doing things the right way' and maintaining order, which sounds principled rather than territorial

Could be mistyped as Type 6 because his vigilance about loyalty, group dynamics, and who can be trusted might read as anxiety-driven threat-scanning rather than the Eight's core drive to dominate and avoid being controlled
